If we were to write an opinion that drew -- tried to draw a distinction between the license plates on one side and those other things on the other side, what would we say?

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance:
Sure, Justice Alito.

I think the very first thing is Texas has its name on it.

I'm not sure in the billboard example --

turns.speaker: Samuel A. Alito, Jr.
turns.utterance: Texas has its name on all the other things, too.

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance:
In this situation, we have exercised selectivity and control as my previous answer to Justice Kagan suggested.

Our reply brief at pages 9 to 11 addresses that.

Also, we market this program to the public saying specifically that no one is entitled to whatever design they want; rather, the board of the legislature has to approve it.

That's at the final item of the Joint Appendix.

So this is not a situation where out in the world if you were to see a soapbox in a park, that you would wonder is this the government speaking? Is this not the government speaking? Is the government abridging traditional free speech rights? This is a case where Texas wants to maintain and has maintained control of what it says on license plates.

And Respondents -- everyone remains free to speak in all sorts of ways.

Speeches, leafletting, TV, advertisements.

They're on billboards.

turns.speaker: Sonia Sotomayor
turns.utterance:
I really don't think that you've answered Justice Alito's question.

In every park you need, generally, a permit to do certain kinds of speech.

So the government controls that permit process, and it tells you that it can say no. So why is that different in the situations that -- it can't be merely control is what I'm saying.

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance: Justice --

turns.speaker: Sonia Sotomayor
turns.utterance: The ability to veto because that would then give you the ability to veto. You could create a program in every public forum that basically controls in the same way.

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance:
Justice Sotomayor, I think there -- there's a difference -- we need to be clear about what approval means.

If approval means access to a forum and it's not government controlling every single word of the message, then I don't think you have government speech.

If it's simply -- you have a permit to --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: Have we held that you can -- you can deny access to a park or to a forum on the basis of the content of the speech?

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance: Justice Scalia, content-based regulations of speech --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance:
Absolutely wrong.

And you're denying access on the basis of content; right? It's a different -- different situation entirely.

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance:
Justice Scalia, that is correct.

We are -- we are denying access.

turns.speaker: Elena Kagan
turns.utterance:
But Mr. Keller, one of the -- one of the concerns that that raises, and this really goes back to what Justice Kennedy said, is that outside the traditional area of streets and parks, this is a new world.

There are all kinds of new expressive forums being created every day and as those come into play, as long as the State says, hey, look, we're going to regulate everything for offense, we're going to keep anything offensive out of this expressive forum.

It does create the possibility that in this new world with all these new kinds of expressive fora, the State will have a much greater control over its citizens' speech than we've typically been comfortable with.

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance: That's right, Justice Kagan. And I think for all of those reasons, a narrow ruling in this case would possibly be a beneficial way to go, but that --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance:
Do -- do you know of any other expressive fora that are owned by the State that, are manufactured by the State that have the State's name on it as license plates do? I mean, if there are a lot of fora like that, boy, I -- I wouldn't really worry. But I don't know of any others.

Do you know of any others?

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance:
No, Justice Scalia.

This is -- this is a unique --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
What can you tell me, then, to help me which might not help others? But I don't think these categories are absolute.

I think they help, but they're not absolute.

So I would ask the question first, this isn't government speech in common English. It is the speech of the person who wants to put the message on the plate.

The plate's owned by the State. The State says we don't want certain messages to be displayed.

And my question is why? Why not? What is the interest that the State is furthering in keeping certain messages off the plate?

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance: Justice Breyer, the State's interest is selecting the messages it -- it wants to put its --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
I'm sorry.

If the State -- then you have the Republican example, Democrats.

I mean, not every interest is a justifiable interest.

Some are not, and some are.

That's why I asked my question. They keep some off, and they let some on. What is their interest? Why -- which are the ones? I'm asking a factual question.

Why have they kept off the ones they kept off while letting on the ones they left on?

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance: Justice Breyer --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
If they have no interest at all in making such a distinction, then I think since speech is hurt at least a little, they ought to lose. But if they have a justifiable interest, since you can put the bumper sticker next door, I think they win.

And therefore, I'd like to know what their interest is.

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance: And the State of Texas interest here is propagating messages that show the diverse backgrounds, educational backgrounds, products of Texas.

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance: Well, yes -- I --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance:
No.

I'm asking the interest of --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
I assume that Texas likes each one of these interests that they allow to be put on the -- on -- on the license plate.

They like Texas hamburger joints, and they probably would not approve a -- a Chicago hamburger joint being on the Texas license plate.

They -- they -- they like some of these messages; others they don't particularly like.

Isn't that right?

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance: Justice --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance: I'd like to get my answer. I was asking you what is the interest in Texas, and why does it keep off the messages it keeps off?

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance: In this particular example --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
No.

Not just this example. There are a set of things they've kept off.

Why?

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance: Justice Breyer --

turns.speaker: Sonia Sotomayor
turns.utterance:
Now, don't try a general rule.

I think the Justice is asking you for a specific. Why would you --

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance:
Justice Breyer, I'll use the example of the Texas DPS Troopers Foundation plate that was also denied.

There, Texas didn't want that on its license plate because it was concerned that if a motorist were pulled over, that then the --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
Okay.

So go through this. Look, I can think of many reasons I could make up. Maybe they want to keep controversial political messages off.

I'd say they have an interest in that in suggesting to people Texas doesn't sponsor this -- I just want to know what -- what they really are.

And now you've said one -- what was the one you just said?

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance: The Texas DPS Troopers Foundation plate, Justice Breyer.

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance:
Well, I'm interested in Justice Breyer's question.

You're on the license plate approval board.

What standard do you follow? When do you grant a request, and when do you deny it? What is the rule? I think that's what Justice Breyer is asking.

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance:
Yes.

And Texas regulations provide that the board can deny a license plate for something that members of the public would find offensive, but it also says that the board can deny plates for any reason established by a rule, which is --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
Fine.

Then I think they lose.

The reason I think they lose is because I don't see a State could come in and say we keep off a private message, and we'll tell you the reason later. We'll -- we can do it for any reason we want.

There you're hurting speech, and I don't see Texas's interest in saying we can keep it off for any reason we want because that would be the Republican-Democrat, too.

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance: Well, but Justice --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
So what's the -- I'm -- same question, but I just think you have to have some kind of legitimate reason for keeping off the -- and it doesn't have to be much.

It could be just a little.

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance: Well, Texas can have legitimate reasons for not allowing --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance: Then why don't you tell us what they are?

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance: Well, I think, though, that that would require something like a formal process and Summum --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
No, it doesn't.

I just want to know what they are.

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance:
And Justice Breyer, Texas does not have to associate itself with messages that it doesn't want to and finds offensive.

And because Texas has given that explanation here, we know that.

But many times government officials speak and -- and they don't disclose their motives, and that's perfectly --

turns.speaker: Ruth Bader Ginsburg
turns.utterance:
But here, Texas -- Texas did.

And now, we get full circle back to the -- my first question. Texas didn't just say no.

It said this message would be offensive to many people.

So that's -- if -- if a message would be offensive to many people, that's a standard that they're applying.

And I asked isn't that too broad a discretion?

turns.speaker: Scott A. Keller
turns.utterance:
No, Justice Ginsburg.

The fact that we have that much discretion confirms that this is government speech. Mr. Chief Justice, let me remain -- reserve the remainder of my time.

turns.speaker: John G. Roberts, Jr.
turns.utterance: Thank you, Counsel. Mr. George.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: We're here representing the Sons of the Confederate Veterans because they wanted to have a license plate to raise money, in fact, for the State of Texas to keep up monuments, which was the purpose of their whole process in this case.

And the State of Texas has gone about issuing an open invitation to everybody to submit to them public designs for license plates and to create -- and thus, it's created a limited public forum for these license plates.

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: Can Texas -- can Texas itself formally, let's say, by a -- a joint resolution of the legislature endorse the Grand Army of the Republic and not the Sons of the Confederacy? Can Texas do that?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: The legislature can endorse anything it wants.

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: So the State can; right? So -- so can the -- can the legislature endorse Austin hamburgers?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Well, the Legislature has created a Confederate Heroes Day in --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: And --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: -- in this particular case. And the people on my side -- this side of the --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: What about Yankee heroes? Are they --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: They -- they -- they --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: -- are they honored in Texas?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: -- do -- well, they created a holiday for --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: To --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: -- people -- for Juneteenth when the slaves were freed.

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: What I don't understand is why this sticks in your craw when it's on -- on a license plate --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: The --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance:
-- when you acknowledge that Texas can do all of these things so long as it's Texas speech.

The only question here is whether this is Texas's speech or not.

If it is Texas's speech, all of these things can be said, can't they? Can't all of the things --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: If it --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: -- that are on the license plates --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: If it's Texas speech by itself --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: It --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: -- and is not joint speech because of --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: Right.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: -- the location.

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: This doesn't seem to me like a very significant issue --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Well --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: -- if that's all you are concerned about.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Well, the --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: So long as Texas says it's -- it's okay, but, boy, if you put it on a license plate, that -- what -- I don't understand what the theory is.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
Well, the State has created a very successful money-raising program in which it solicits people to come in and submit their design for their license plate so they can -- they have to submit the design.

They have to put up the money to make the -- put the -- a plate.

And then the plate doesn't ever get published to anybody until the person -- somebody orders it from the --

turns.speaker: Ruth Bader Ginsburg
turns.utterance:
Suppose -- suppose the message -- the -- the applicant said, we want this design, and the design is a swastika.

Is that speech that -- does -- does the -- the -- whoever is in charge of it -- of the license plate, do they have to accept --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: I don't --

turns.speaker: Ruth Bader Ginsburg
turns.utterance: -- that -- that design?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: I don't believe the State can discriminate against the people who want to have that design --

turns.speaker: Ruth Bader Ginsburg
turns.utterance:
So they could have the swastika.

And suppose somebody else says, I want to have "Jihad" on my license plate.

That's okay, too?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Vegan?

turns.speaker: Ruth Bader Ginsburg
turns.utterance: Jihad.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
Jihad.

Jihad on the license plate? Can be -- there is obviously a court of appeal -- a district court from Ohio in which "Infidels" was held to be -- the State --

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance: What is your answer --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: -- wouldn't put that.

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance: What is your answer in this case as to Justice Ginsburg's hypothetical? Yes or no, must the State put those symbols or messages on the plates at the request of the citizen? Yes or no?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Yes.

turns.speaker: Ruth Bader Ginsburg
turns.utterance: How about "Make pot legal."

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Say it again.

turns.speaker: Ruth Bader Ginsburg
turns.utterance: "Make pot legal."

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Yes.

turns.speaker: Ruth Bader Ginsburg
turns.utterance: That's okay? And "Bong hits for Jesus"? (Laughter.)

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Yes.

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: So you're -- you're really arguing for the abolition for Texas specialty plates, aren't you?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: I am arguing that if the State --

turns.speaker: Antonin Scalia
turns.utterance: I couldn't make a better argument for -- in that direction than -- than what you've been doing.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Well, we had got along without it a long time before we got it, and we can get along --

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance: So -- so in a way --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: -- without it again.

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance: So in a way, your argument curtails speech?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Only if --

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance: If you prevail, you are going to prevent a lot of Texans from -- from conveying a message -- you have to agree with that.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
I would -- if -- if the -- if the State continues to use the same standard, which is it might offend anybody, the State can -- can deny the plate.

If that's the standard, then the -- and -- and they exercise the discretion on the statutory standard that it might offend somebody --

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance: But you have no alternate standard in order to have a proper -- or solution that seems -- seems wise for Justice Ginsburg's hypothetical. You have no standard.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: I -- the -- the answer to having a standard that controls people's speech is that the standard has to be pretty low-hanging fruit. As -- in the Christian Law Students Association, College of Hastings, v. Martinez, Justice Alito, in the dissent for the dissenters in that case said that offensive speech is something that -- speech that we hate is something that we should be proud of protecting.

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
Yeah, that's in that context.

So you -- but I'm trying -- you say they can or they cannot have a standard which says we're trying to keep offensive speech off the license plate?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: I -- as long as the --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance: You say yes or no?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: -- definition of offensive is in the eyes of the beholder --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance: Yeah.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: -- of course.

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance: They can do that?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: They can't have that.

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance: They can or cannot?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Cannot.

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
Okay.

So now I see what you're saying.

But if I were to go back to sort of the basic underlying thought here, is speech hurt?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Yes.

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
The answer is, yes, it is. The private speech is somewhat hurt.

A lot? Well, put up a bumper sticker.

You can't say a lot.

How is it hurt? You don't get the official imprimatur.

Hmm, okay. Now, is there something to be said for Texas? Yes.

What they're trying to do is to prevent their official imprimatur from being given to speech that offends people.

People don't like it? Put up a bumper sticker. All right.

Now we have two interests in opposite directions in many, many cases.

We try to weigh those things if the other things don't tell us the answer.

And I would guess -- I don't really see the big problem -- that people who are putting up speech even that Texas considers offensive, in part, for reasons that Justice Scalia says, well, put up a bumper sticker. What's the problem?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
Well, the -- I -- the culture of creating specialty plates began in Texas in 1965. We've been doing this, and we have gone bonkers with people buying these things in the State.

There are 50,000 people with -- with the private plates --

turns.speaker: Sonia Sotomayor
turns.utterance: There's a lot of money in this, isn't it? It's about $8,000?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Say what?

turns.speaker: Sonia Sotomayor
turns.utterance: Is it about $8,000 to get one of these plates?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: I think it's a little more than that.

turns.speaker: Sonia Sotomayor
turns.utterance: More than that.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: And that -- that --

turns.speaker: Sonia Sotomayor
turns.utterance:
I have -- I -- I have a different question, which is -- I actually do think that this is hybrid speech.

It's both government and the individual speaking at the same time.

But that goes back to what Justice Scalia said.

In Wooley we said we can't compel the individual to put something on their license plates that they disagree with.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: That's -- we had that case.

turns.speaker: Sonia Sotomayor
turns.utterance: So why isn't the reverse true for the government? If you're going to ask me to put my name, because the law requires it, the State's name on a license plate, why can you compel us to do something we don't want to endorse?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Well, the reason --

turns.speaker: Sonia Sotomayor
turns.utterance: Why shouldn't it work both ways when it's --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: The reason is that this has become -- and it's the numbers -- it's become a limited public forum for putting up messages.

turns.speaker: Sonia Sotomayor
turns.utterance:
But how -- how do I know which is the government's and which is only the individual's? I mean, I -- I -- I wouldn't have known that pro -- that -- that pro anything was sponsored by some States and not others, or endorsed by some States but not others.

So how do I know that a particular license plate the government doesn't endorse?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: You can't tell whether the government wants your speech in advance in this program. You have to submit what you think you want, and then the --

turns.speaker: Sonia Sotomayor
turns.utterance: Well, that implies a certain degree of approval.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
And these -- well, of course, there is approval.

Just like there's approval for someone to speak in a park, the -- in the Columbus, Ohio case where the --

turns.speaker: Ruth Bader Ginsburg
turns.utterance:
But that's -- that's -- I think was brought out earlier.

You can have time, place, and manner regulations for speaking in the park. You can't have content-based regulation.

This is -- this is a content basis, content the State doesn't want.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
The -- and they have.

They have a standard that is -- that -- the lowest common -- common denominator.

If any person could be offended, they can deny it.

That is their standard, which, in fact, is --

turns.speaker: Ruth Bader Ginsburg
turns.utterance:
Well, it wasn't quite that.

It says it would be offensive to many people.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
No, ma'am.

I think the statute is -- it says, actually, any person.

So --

turns.speaker: Elena Kagan
turns.utterance:
Well, of course, Mr. George.

If you had a standard like that in a case, in a normal case where we were regulating private speech, of course, we would find that impermissible. But the question is whether this is a very different kind of context. And let's go back to -- I think Justice Scalia said it, about the nature of license plates.

I mean, I think there is a clear regulatory purpose here. It's the government that actually makes the license plate.

I think the license plate continues to be public property, if that's right, like you have to return the license plate.

It has the State's name on it.

It's clearly the official identification that the State gives with respect to a car. So why doesn't all of that make -- make this a very different case from the typical forum cases that -- that we usually address?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
Well, the reason is that we do have hybrid speech and they opened up, and they created this billboard, as Justice Alito said, they created a billboard opportunity.

And they have -- since they can make everybody have a license plate, they said we're going to create a billboard opportunity and put messages -- let you put messages on it and pay us money for using our billboard.

That's what they've done.

And then they say to some people, but if I don't like your message because you're a Republican or you're a Democrat or you are -- you want to say Mighty fine burgers instead of Whopper Burger, they can do that. That sort of arbitrary control of speech based upon a standard that it might offend anybody is they either need to get rid of the program, or they need to open up the program just to everybody else.

And if somebody publishes a speech they don't like, Justice O'Connor in the Columbus, Ohio case suggested you just make them put a number under it whom the Ku Klux Klan puts a cross on the hill in Columbus, Ohio.

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance: I asked my question before, if you remember it, really, because I wanted an answer.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: I'll try again.

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
It wasn't a statement.

It was -- it was -- I'm trying to get rid of all the conceptual basis here.

You just go back -- forget the public forum, et cetera, forget all that.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Okay.

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
Just go back to look to see is speech being hurt, and the answer is of course yes, but not much, because they can put a bumper sticker. Then you look at the other side of it, and you say, does the State have a legitimate interest here? And the State says yes, our interest is that there are messages we like, messages we don't care about, and messages we don't like.

And do we have a system for keeping the last off because it is the government speaking, which represents the citizens, and the citizens do -- its their government, and they don't want, just as in the examples Justice Scalia gave, to have their government associated with messages that this commission doesn't want.

And maybe there are limits on that, but that's the basic idea.

Okay.

Now, weigh those two things.

I think you'd say, little harm to speech, we see the other broad, da, da, da, et cetera.

Now, what's your response?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Well, the response is the forum has been created.

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
Forum.

See, it's the conceptual part, and I can't tell whether a forum -- a license plate's a forum or not a forum, or if it's a three-part test or -- I can't get that.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: I understand.

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance: I'm trying to go back to the basics of it.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
Well, one of the -- one of the ideas that you have articulated, and others on this Court, is that what would the reasonable observer believe this was.

For example, the -- would they believe that the speech is the State's speech or would they believe it's the person who bought the plate, because there's no -- nothing gets communicated --

turns.speaker: Ruth Bader Ginsburg
turns.utterance:
How about both? How about both in answer to that? It is the State's license plate.

It has Texas on it in big letters, so -- and Texas says, yes, we have to approve it, yes, we approve a lot, but there's some we don't approve, because it's our speech, it may be the car owner's speech as well, but it is our speech.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
Both -- the State has dozens of potential designs for plates that don't carry anybody else's message, and they have 480 designs for organizational messages and 50,000 personalized messages.

And the issue in this case is the person who puts the license plates on their car is the one that communicates the message.

The other people are just giving approval.

turns.speaker: Samuel A. Alito, Jr.
turns.utterance:
Suppose the State had many fewer plates from which to choose.

So let's say they have the standard plate, and then they have a plate for every college or university in the State.

That's your choice.

Would that be government speech?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
It certainly is government speech in the sense that -- partly government speech, the ability to choose somebody -- the universities in the State.

And if the standard by which they issue those is that we're going to put one for all the colleges in the State, and that is the standard, of course that's okay.

Because it is a -- a standard that has -- they chose or the legislature chose, I suspect, that says you can have -- everybody who has a college can get into this program.

turns.speaker: Samuel A. Alito, Jr.
turns.utterance:
All right.

Suppose they broaden it.

So it's not only the colleges and universities, but it's all the places in Texas of historic interest or natural features of the State.

How about that? Now you've got a lot more.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
They actually do those and those are not sponsored by anybody.

Those are State-created for -- and they charge more money --

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance:
But answer -- answer the hypothetical.

Justice Alito says first colleges and the next scenic places.

That's an okay or not?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Scenic places, they can -- they actually --

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance: And then I'm sure he has some other --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: -- they can and do.

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance: All right.

turns.speaker: Samuel A. Alito, Jr.
turns.utterance:
And suppose that there's some little town that thinks that it's really scenic and they -- there's a way in which they can petition to get on this list.

Do you see where I'm going? At some point, if it's -- if you have just a standard State plate, of course that's government speech.

If you've got 5,000 different variations that people can create for themselves, it becomes a lot harder to say that's government speech.

So where would you draw the line?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: My view is that when the people get to create a message themselves, and then -- an organization in this case -- create the message for themselves, and then the people who look in the catalog, pick out the license plate that they want and put it on their car, then the speech is the speech of the person who communicated it is predominantly --

turns.speaker: Sonia Sotomayor
turns.utterance:
My -- my problem with this is, how do I know? There are three categories of plates, I understand.

There's the official State plate, there are specialty plates created by the legislature, and there are specialty plates created by an individual. How do I tell the difference between the legislative plates, which are government speech, and the private plates? Do I need to? What I do know is what I said at the beginning, it's both people speaking.

And I think both people endorsing each other's message in some way.

So why should the government be compelled to accept speech it rejects because it thinks it's wrong?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: In the first place --

turns.speaker: Sonia Sotomayor
turns.utterance: And doesn't want to be associated with directly.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
I understand.

In the first place, the way people pick out plates, there's a big long catalog with 400 different organizational plates, 480 now, and it grows every day, of organizational plates.

And people pick them out of -- on -- out of a catalog out of a website, and they pick the one they want to pick, and then they put it on their license plate.

The communication of the information on the license plates actually is controlled entirely by the people who pick the plates.

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance: But what about Justice Alito's hypothetical -- we never did quite finish -- where the direction of his question was, suppose the State, all by itself, has 10 messages, 20 messages, 200 messages, 2000 messages, you can choose, but the State makes up all the messages and gives you all the choices. What results?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Well, the result is if the State has -- controls all -- has all the messages and picks all the messages, and then the people from whom -- whom it picks -- who it sells the plates to --

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance:
I know that's the result of the hypothetical.

I want to know the legal result. What's the First Amendment answer?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Well, the State can design all kinds of license plates that it wants to choose --

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance: Is the scheme that I proposed, and that followed from Justice Alito's questions, consistent with the First Amendment or not?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: My -- it is not First -- when the individual submits -- when people, other people submit the design --

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance: That's not the hypothetical.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: I understand that.

turns.speaker: Anthony M. Kennedy
turns.utterance:
The hypothetical is the State has 5,000 designs, and the State makes them all up, and you can choose.

Is there a First Amendment violation?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: I don't believe -- if the State does everything, then it's -- it's the creator of the message and the speaker is the driver.

turns.speaker: Elena Kagan
turns.utterance: What happens if private people could submit messages, but they all had to go through the legislature?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: My view is that it is a much more difficult case for us if the legislature passes a statute, because that is a legislative act and a clear act of the State.

turns.speaker: Elena Kagan
turns.utterance: Well, what's the difference, then? I mean, if you say -- if you think that that would be all right, you know, Texas has said, well, the DMV does it, not the legislature, a different branch of government, but it's government just the same.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
I understand that.

And the -- and the issue is whether or not the -- in the cases -- we have court of appeals cases that don't distinguish between legislative action and non-legislative actions, and those that do. It is my judgment that the State has a greater claim making its speech when every -- when the legislature passes the bill and the governor signs it, then the statute is clearly an explanation -- or expression of the State.

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance: Well, if you --

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: What regard when anybody buys a license plate --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
When -- in Wooley, go back to that for a second.

I take it that if I object to the message on the New Hampshire plate, Live Free of Die, I have a right to be disassociated with that.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Yes.

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
Okay.

Well, if the State, which represents many people in Texas, doesn't want to be associated with the particular message, why doesn't it have the right to say we don't want that event, we don't want that association.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: Because --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance:
I mean, the State represents X million people.

They don't want to be associated with this message through their official organ.

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
I understand.

Quite frankly --

turns.speaker: Stephen G. Breyer
turns.utterance: What's the difference?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance:
The difference is they invite people to make their -- they charge people and have them pay for the manufacture of the license plates by giving them the chance to design a message.

That's what they do.

They -- it costs the people who come up with these things, they pay all the front-end costs, put up $8,000 collateral before any license plate is built, and it is a money-making scheme that they use.

The fact that they choose to, apparently twice in history -- and there may be more, but I, we can't document anymore -- they've ever turned anybody down, this is not a forum which people actually -- they make any decision besides an economic decision.

It's a factual matter; that's what happens.

turns.speaker: John G. Roberts, Jr.
turns.utterance:
Counsel, can I ask you, it's a somewhat technical question but you just touched on it.

Do you have an objection to the materials that your friend has cited from outside the record?

turns.speaker: Roger James George, Jr.
turns.utterance: I -- to the extent he's cited issues relating to the other design, I do not have an objection to that, because I think it's --

turns.speaker: John G. Roberts, Jr.
turns.utterance: You think that it -- it's the extrarecord materials are accurate --

turns.utterance: For me, faster yeah.

turns.speaker: Thy
turns.utterance: What about the fact that some people tell me “writing I must think, then if it’s proper then I write, if not I end up using correction tape and all.” But typing you and cut and paste and move things around. Is that one of the factors?

turns.speaker: Jayden
turns.utterance: I mean for me it doesn’t really affect me that much, because I don’t really think I just write. Whatever pops up in my mind I just write. But after checking then I will do the corrections, like I said change the words type and everything.

turns.speaker: Thy
turns.utterance: Sure. So as you say, obviously after you’ve done that, digitally is easier to correct compared to [paper]?

turns.speaker: Jayden
turns.utterance: Yeah because you need to use correction tape which spends money and another thing is that you have no space. If you cancel you have no space, you realise you have no space.

turns.speaker: Thy
turns.utterance: Okay so I can see that that’s the advantage you were saying. Okay let’s talk about the part where after you’ve submitted your work, then what happens after the teacher here returns you the work versus in school? My impression from school is that teacher just brings out a few examples and-

turns.speaker: Jayden
turns.utterance:
I mean it’s definitely faster, 100%. To my school teacher’s credit, she make the effort to mark one by one our work, and then actually brings out the top scorer’s work and give it to us. Like you said rough examples and ask you to basically learn from them.

But then after that you see WA2 they fail. I would say Scribo is very fast I guess because of the help of technology also? And it spots all your mistakes.

turns.speaker: Thy
turns.utterance: So you know immediately...okay.

turns.speaker: Jayden
turns.utterance: Yeah you basically don’t really need a teacher? But some things still need a teacher, some things.

turns.speaker: Thy
turns.utterance: Okay. Are you required to do corrections for school?

turns.speaker: Jayden
turns.utterance: No. Somehow I don’t know why, I also thought I need to do corrections but they just basically flash the top scorers paper, and then tell us just look at that, and then that’s it. That’s your correction, but they print out the paper for you already.

turns.speaker: Thy
turns.utterance: Okay. But over here, you’re obviously...

turns.speaker: Jayden
turns.utterance: Yeah you need, you do some adjustments and everything. Teacher also went-

turns.speaker: Thy
turns.utterance: He goes through and after he goes through and gives comments, are you required to re-draft?

turns.speaker: Jayden
turns.utterance: Yeah change it and he also helps us a lot with different things.

turns.speaker: Thy
turns.utterance: Okay. So I can see how between school and here there’s a difference in the preparation in the writing as well as the...I’m so reluctant to let you go. Okay I’m gonna ask you 1 or 2 last questions. If I were to ask you, are you more confident in English language now? Would you agree? Disagree?

turns.speaker: Jayden
turns.utterance: Yeah definitely. Definitely more confident I would say speaking-wise. Because Scribo helped me on writing, I feel like after writing you can actually convert those into speaking. You know more vocab, you know what to say. sentence don’t sound so boring.

turns.speaker: Thy
turns.utterance: Okay. So you’re more confident, let’s say going forward, have you changed the way you approach your learning of English language? I mean previously students who tell me “cher English cannot study one.”

turns.speaker: Jayden
turns.utterance:
I mean wouldn’t say I changed that much, but I would say I actually read more because I just like reading now. I got into reading. Parents always called me for not reading the news so had no choice. Basically that’s really it.

I mean English...I would say using Scribo is definitely easier to spot your mistakes without your teacher. But you got to be responsible enough.

turns.speaker: Thy
turns.utterance: That’s great, I think such a good model for the success of the program, especially after you say you’ve benefited from it from one year. We wanted to know if after one year, is there a plateau or does it keep going up. So what do you think? Go up come down, go up plateau, or go up and keep on going up?

turns.speaker: Jayden
turns.utterance: Slight increase? Because I can say I think it needs a little bit more adjustment to actually help the students because sometimes it’s [inaudible], when you don’t want to change the word it give you less marks et cetera.

turns.utterance: Okay thank you Jayden you have been very helpful!

metadata.description: Randolph (Randy) Adams served as a Peace Corps Volunteer in the Dominican Republic from 1966 to 1969. He worked on community development projects in El Guanal and other towns, then continued for a third year in the education unit of a newly created agricultural reform agency in addition to training new volunteers. Beginning in the 1980s, Adams resumed his affiliation with the Peace Corps and worked in a contract position as a trainer in Venezuela and Peru, then moved to agency headquarters where he filled various positions, including Director of Evaluation (2006-2011). In total, he held 12 different positions. During his interview, Adams talks about changes in volunteer training practices over the decades, from the university-based training in the U.S. and his "outward bound" training in Puerto Rico, to in-country training and home stays for cultural immersion. He discusses how the Peace Corps was generally trusted by various factions during the post-dictator period, but also mentions the bombing of the agency's offices after an American columnist (Drew Pearson) accused the Peace Corps of being affiliated with the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA). Adams talks about the impact of the Peace Corps, both in terms of "capacity-building" in foreign countries by helping people learn how to solve their own problems, and within the U.S. as returned volunteers share their deeper understanding of the world. He also discusses the important role of host country Peace Corps staff and his belief that the "5-year rule" that limits their terms leads to administrative inefficiencies and a lack of continuity. Adams is on the board of the National Peace Corps Association (NPCA) and concludes the interview with comments about the organization's evolving role and activities. Interviewed and recorded by Evelyn Ganzglass, August 15, 2018. 2 digital files (web streaming files combined into 1 file).
metadata.copyright_status: Public Domain (Donated to the United States Government)
metadata.preferred_citation: Returned Peace Corps Volunteer Collection. Dominican Republic. Adams, Randolph (Randy) (1966-1969): Oral history interview
